Gentlemen;
i am writing to reqwcst your opinion of a proposed course of
action by PASPAC—Califaxnia which is a political action committee sponsored
by The E] raso Campully and subsidiary and affiliated companies.
The
committee has rcpoctcd to your iurisdiction.
According to its bylaws,
it is involved only in nontederal chlitornia elections.
our company also
sponsors other committees. each oi which is involved in different kinds
oi elections in other jurisdictions.
Many of our employccs contriowte to our committees by the
payroll deduction method oascd on written deduction requests signed by
the employees.
hsch deduction rcqucst spccitics the total amount which
each employee wishes to cuntribuce to all of nur political action
committees contined. and the deduction request also indicates the
percentage of the total which the employee wishes to go to each of our
committees. including PASPAC-Califarnla.
For many reasons. it is desired
to achieve ilexioility concerning the percentage oi each employee's
total contrioution which goes to each of our committees. including
Phsrdc-Caliiornia.
in order to avoid the necessity ior repeated execution
of new payroll deduction requests, we propose to suggesc to our employees
who use the payroll deduction systan that they execute a written document
apponuing a specific. person in our company as their agent tor the
apportionment of their total contributions among our various committees.
Under this optional agency document, the agent will he atplicitly authorized
by the employee to change the apportionment of his total contrioution
frau- time to time according to the present needs of our various committees.
Each contrioucor will he advised, via our Committee newsletter, when the
apportionment is about to he changed by the agent.
The contributor will
also he informed of the new percentages which will determine how much oi

will be given a statement detailing how much he or she contributed to
our various committees during the year.
Al. any time during the year the
employee can determine how much he or she has contributed to each of our
committees by simply contacting the treasurer of our committees.
The
document authorizing the agent to apportion contributions can be revoked
at any time.
This new device of an agent for apportionment of contribucions
would be offered to cur employees who use the payroll deduction system
for their contributionst
it will be entirely optional.
Employees could
still designate iixed percentages at their total contribution to go to

or they may elect not to use the payroll deduc-
tion system at all: instead they may contribute by separate checks from

of coorse. an employee may choose not to
contribute at all to our committees.
The agency device that we propose
to offer would simply eliminate the necessity of suggesting to our
employees that they erecuts. repeatedly, new payroll deduction requests
in response to the changing relative financial needs of our several
comiEEEesa
we would appreciate your opinion stating whether this proposal
would create any problems under your state election law.
we have
researched the law. and we cannot find any problems with our proposal.
We would appreciate your opinion as soon as possible.
Thank you very
much.
sincerely,
hemry L. Smith. .vrr

sections.question: You asked whether the proposed method would allow El Paso compliance with reporting requirements for recipient committees under the Political Reform Act of 1974.
sections.conclusion: I see no anticipated problems with the proposed method of deduction or record keeping for contributions from employees of an El Paso Company.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether a proposed payroll deduction method with an agent authorized to apportion employee contributions among multiple political action committees would comply with Political Reform Act reporting requirements for recipient committees.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC found no compliance problems with the proposed payroll deduction and apportionment system, including the use of an agent to allocate contributions among multiple political action committees.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains both the incoming request (dated March 9, 1981) and FPPC response (dated April 5, 1981). Some OCR quality issues but core Q/C sections identifiable. Year discrepancy noted (document header says 1951 but content clearly from 1981). Response letter is brief without detailed analysis.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: You asked whether the proposed method would allow El Paso compliance with reporting requirements for recipient committees under the Political Reform Act of 1974.

CONCLUSION: I see no anticipated problems with the proposed method of deduction or record keeping for contributions from employees of an El Paso Company.

embedding.summary: The FPPC advised that El Paso Company's proposed method allowing employees to authorize a payroll deduction with an agent managing the apportionment of contributions among multiple political action committees presents no anticipated problems under the Political Reform Act of 1974.
